question_text: During a full Moon, the Moon, Earth, and Sun are arranged in a straight line with Earth in the middle. Explain why we see the fully illuminated face of the Moon at this time, and predict what would happen to tides during a full Moon compared to a quarter Moon. Justify your answer with reference to the Moon's gravitational pull.
correct_answer: During a full Moon, the Sun and Moon are on opposite sides of Earth, so the entire sunlit side of the Moon faces us, making it fully illuminated. Tides during a full Moon are higher (spring tides) because both the Sun and Moon's gravity pull on Earth's oceans in the same direction, creating a stronger combined gravitational effect. During a quarter Moon, the Sun and Moon are at right angles to Earth, so their gravitational pulls partially cancel out, resulting in smaller tides (neap tides).
explanation: This question requires students to combine understanding of moon phases (relative positions of Sun, Earth, Moon), tidal mechanics (Moon's gravity effect), and to explain cause-and-effect relationships. A strong answer should explain the geometry of the full Moon phase, describe spring vs neap tides, and connect tidal differences to the direction and strength of gravitational pulls.
